metadata.title: Claude, On Average
metadata.color_semantics: claude: INK #3D3929 all text; SPARK #D97757 the one accent; callout ink #161511 on cream; code stays Onda dark glass
metadata.note: Audience-namespaced adaptation. B08 defines when generative variance helps and when AI should not be relied upon; B09 is the audience outro.

beats.narration_text: Ask the same question twice and something curious happens: Claude may answer differently. The words in the prompt have not moved, yet the result has. That variation is not an accident around the system. It is a clue to what the system is.

props.command: why did I get a different answer this time?

beats.narration_text: Run one prompt three times. Three plausible lines appear, each traveling through the same idea by a different path. A database would retrieve the same stored row. Claude is doing something stranger: each response is one draw from a landscape of possible continuations.

props.title: same prompt, three draws
props.code:
$ claude -p "write one line about focus"
▸ Focus is the art of saying no.

$ claude -p "write one line about focus"
▸ Attention narrows. That's how you get anywhere.

$ claude -p "write one line about focus"
▸ What you choose not to do is the strategy.

beats.narration_text: It is tempting to imagine a hidden correct answer waiting to be retrieved. Repetition breaks that picture. There may be many defensible continuations and no single privileged sentence. What remains stable is not the wording of an answer, but the distribution from which answers emerge.

At each step, the model estimates what token could follow everything already present: the prompt, the system instructions, and its own growing response. Then the new token becomes part of the context for the next estimate. An answer is assembled one conditional step at a time, not fetched whole from storage.

Temperature zero chooses the most probable continuation at each step, but probability still depends on context. Change the persona or surrounding instructions and the most probable path can change too. Lower temperature narrows the wandering. It does not turn generation into a pinned script.

Here the apparent defect becomes useful. Variation lets the model approach a brief from several directions. Some draws are ordinary; some reveal a connection we did not think to request. We should not romanticize the tails, but exploration would be poorer without them.

The same variation carries a cost. One draw cannot certify a fact merely because it sounds complete. When two runs must agree exactly, generation is the wrong mechanism. Use code, a fixed rule, or a gate. Let the model propose possibilities; let evidence decide what survives.

props.sparkLine: One draw is not the answer.

beats.narration_text: Claude is best understood as a distribution shaped by context. Temperature changes how narrowly we sample it. Skills and prompts bias the region we visit. Scripts pin what must be exact. Gates reject outputs that fail explicit tests. Once we see the distribution, the rest of the architecture begins to make sense.

artifactLines: You're not querying a database. You're sampling a distribution.
Same prompt, different draw — that's not a bug, that's the definition.
Temperature zero steers toward most likely; it does not specify an output.
Variance is the creativity. Certify draws with gates, not with faith.
Scripts pin the exact. Skills bias the draw. Gates catch the bad draws.

beats.narration_text: When should we invite this variation? Use AI when several possible answers help us explore, compare, or discover. Do not use it where a person needs the same verified result every time. There, replace sampling with a calculation, a source, or a rule. Possibility is valuable only when we remember that it is not proof.

props.artifactTitle: When variation helps — and when it harms
artifactLines: USE AI — exploration · alternatives · drafts · hypotheses
DO NOT RELY ON A DRAW — facts · eligibility · safety · exact repetition

beats.narration_text: Follow the question further at Medhavy.com.
